Thermodynamics.skill: Kinetic Theory of Temperature and Pressure
Thermodynamics.prompt: This topic covers kinetic theory of temperature and pressure for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that temperature is a measure of the average translational kinetic energy of particles: KE_avg = (3/2)*k_B*T, where k_B is Boltzmann's constant and T is absolute temperature in Kelvin. Pressure results from collisions of gas molecules with container walls. Higher temperature means faster particles and greater pressure (for a fixed volume). The root-mean-square speed relates to temperature: v_rms = sqrt(3*k_B*T/m). At the same temperature, lighter molecules move faster. Students must relate macroscopic quantities (P, T) to microscopic particle behavior and interpret Maxwell-Boltzmann distributions. Connect to ideal gas law, thermodynamic processes, and entropy. AP exam note: connecting microscopic and macroscopic descriptions is essential. Vocabulary: kinetic theory, temperature, average kinetic energy, Boltzmann constant, pressure, rms speed, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution.

This topic covers the ideal gas law for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that PV = nRT = Nk_BT relates macroscopic state variables: pressure (P), volume (V), temperature (T), and amount of gas (n moles or N molecules). R is the universal gas constant and k_B is Boltzmann's constant. The ideal gas model assumes point particles with no intermolecular forces and perfectly elastic collisions. Students must apply the ideal gas law to calculate state variables, analyze changes of state, and use proportional reasoning (e.g., if V doubles at constant T and n, P halves). PV diagrams represent thermodynamic processes graphically. Connect to kinetic theory, thermodynamic processes, and real gas deviations. AP exam note: PV diagram interpretation is a core skill throughout thermodynamics. Vocabulary: ideal gas law, state variable, pressure, volume, temperature, moles, PV diagram, equation of state.

This topic covers thermal energy transfer and equilibrium for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand that thermal energy transfers spontaneously from higher to lower temperature objects via conduction (direct contact, molecular collisions), convection (bulk fluid movement), and radiation (electromagnetic waves). Thermal equilibrium occurs when objects reach the same temperature and net heat flow ceases. The zeroth law of thermodynamics: if A is in thermal equilibrium with C, and B is in thermal equilibrium with C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium. Heat (Q) flows until equilibrium: Q_lost = Q_gained (conservation of energy). Students must identify transfer mechanisms and calculate equilibrium temperatures. Connect to specific heat, first law, and entropy. AP exam note: identifying heat transfer mechanisms and applying conservation of energy are tested. Vocabulary: conduction, convection, radiation, thermal equilibrium, zeroth law, heat transfer, temperature difference, net heat flow.

This topic covers the first law of thermodynamics for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand that the first law is conservation of energy applied to thermodynamic systems: delta-U = Q - W (or Q + W depending on sign convention), where delta-U is change in internal energy, Q is heat added to the system, and W is work done by the system. For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature: delta-U = nCv*delta-T. Work done by a gas: W = integral of P*dV = area under the curve on a PV diagram. Key processes: isothermal (delta-T = 0, delta-U = 0, Q = W), isobaric (constant P, W = P*delta-V), isochoric (constant V, W = 0, delta-U = Q), and adiabatic (Q = 0, delta-U = -W). Students must apply the first law to each process type and calculate W from PV diagrams. AP exam note: PV diagram analysis with the first law is heavily tested. Vocabulary: first law, internal energy, heat, work, isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, adiabatic, PV diagram.

This topic covers specific heat and thermal conductivity for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand that specific heat capacity (c) determines how much heat is needed to change a substance's temperature: Q = mc*delta-T. Different substances have different specific heats (water has a high specific heat, metals generally have low values). Thermal conductivity (k or kappa) measures how well a material conducts heat: the rate of heat flow through a material is Q/t = kA*delta-T/L, where A is cross-sectional area, delta-T is temperature difference, and L is thickness. Good thermal conductors (metals) have high k; good insulators (wood, foam) have low k. Students must calculate heat transfer and compare thermal properties. Connect to calorimetry, energy transfer, and thermodynamic processes. AP exam note: calorimetry calculations using Q = mc*delta-T are commonly tested. Vocabulary: specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, heat transfer rate, insulator, conductor, calorimetry, temperature change.

Thermodynamics.skill: Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics
Thermodynamics.prompt: This topic covers entropy and the second law of thermodynamics for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students should understand that entropy (S) measures the number of microstates available to a system (disorder or energy dispersal). The second law states that the total entropy of an isolated system never decreases: delta-S_universe >= 0. For reversible processes, delta-S = Q/T. Heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold because this increases total entropy. Heat engines convert thermal energy to work with efficiency limited by the Carnot efficiency: e_max = 1 - Tc/Th. No engine can be 100 percent efficient. Refrigerators and heat pumps require work input to move heat from cold to hot. Students must calculate entropy changes, engine efficiency, and apply the second law. Connect to thermodynamic processes, free energy, and spontaneity. AP exam note: entropy concepts and efficiency calculations are tested. Vocabulary: entropy, second law, irreversible, reversible, Carnot efficiency, heat engine, microstate, spontaneous.

Electric Force, Field, and Potential.prompt: This topic covers electric charge and electric force for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that electric charge comes in two types (positive and negative), is quantized (in units of elementary charge e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C), and is conserved. Coulomb's law gives the electrostatic force between two point charges: F = kq1q2/r^2, where k = 8.99 x 10^9 N*m^2/C^2. The force is attractive between unlike charges and repulsive between like charges. The force is along the line connecting the charges and obeys Newton's third law. For multiple charges, the net force is the vector sum of individual Coulombic forces (superposition principle). Students must calculate forces and apply superposition in one and two dimensions. Connect to electric fields, potential energy, and conductors. AP exam note: Coulomb's law calculations and superposition are foundational. Vocabulary: electric charge, Coulomb's law, electrostatic force, elementary charge, superposition, point charge, attractive, repulsive.

Electric Force, Field, and Potential.skill: Conservation of Electric Charge and the Process of Charging
Electric Force, Field, and Potential.prompt: This topic covers conservation of charge and charging processes for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that electric charge is conserved in all processes: charge cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred. Charging methods include friction (triboelectric effect), conduction (direct contact transfers charge), and induction (charge redistribution without contact, using grounding). In conductors, excess charge resides on the surface and distributes to minimize repulsion. Insulators hold charge in place where deposited. Grounding connects an object to a large charge reservoir (Earth), neutralizing it. Polarization occurs when a charged object induces charge separation in a neutral object (explains attraction between charged and neutral objects). Students must explain charging processes and predict charge distributions. AP exam note: conceptual questions on charging methods and conductor behavior are common. Vocabulary: conservation of charge, conduction, induction, friction, grounding, polarization, conductor, insulator.

This topic covers electric fields for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that an electric field E is the force per unit positive test charge: E = F/q = kQ/r^2 for a point charge. Electric field is a vector quantity pointing away from positive charges and toward negative charges. Field lines represent the field: they start on positive charges, end on negative charges, never cross, and their density indicates field strength. For multiple charges, the net field is the vector sum of individual fields (superposition). A uniform electric field exists between parallel plates: E = V/d. The force on a charge in a field is F = qE. Students must calculate fields, draw field lines, and determine forces on charges in fields. Connect to electric potential, Gauss's law concepts, and capacitors. AP exam note: field line diagrams and field calculations are heavily tested. Vocabulary: electric field, field lines, superposition, point charge field, uniform field, force on charge, test charge.

This topic covers electric potential energy for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand that electric potential energy (U) is the energy stored in a configuration of charges: for two point charges, U = kq1q2/r. Positive U (like charges) means repulsive configuration (energy decreases as charges separate). Negative U (unlike charges) means attractive configuration (energy decreases as charges approach). For a charge in a uniform field: delta-U = qE*d (where d is displacement against the field). The work done by the electric force equals the negative change in potential energy: W = -delta-U. Conservation of energy applies: KE + U = constant for charges moving in electric fields. Students must calculate potential energy and apply energy conservation to charged particle motion. Connect to electric potential, work, and kinetic energy. AP exam note: energy conservation with electric PE is commonly tested. Vocabulary: electric potential energy, configuration, Coulomb potential energy, conservative force, work, energy conservation.

This topic covers electric potential for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that electric potential (V) is potential energy per unit charge: V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge. Potential is a scalar quantity (no direction). The potential difference (voltage) between two points determines the work done per unit charge: delta-V = W/q = -integral of E*dr. Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to field lines and connect points of equal potential. The electric field points from high to low potential: E = -dV/dx (field is the negative gradient of potential). In a uniform field: delta-V = -E*d. For multiple point charges, V_total = sum of individual potentials (scalar sum). Students must calculate potentials, relate V to E, and interpret equipotential maps. Connect to potential energy, capacitors, and circuits. AP exam note: relating field to potential is a key conceptual skill. Vocabulary: electric potential, voltage, potential difference, equipotential, volt, scalar, gradient.

This topic covers capacitors for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand that a capacitor stores charge and energy. Capacitance C = Q/V, measured in farads (F). For a parallel-plate capacitor: C = epsilon_0 * A/d, where A is plate area and d is plate separation. Inserting a dielectric (insulator) between plates increases capacitance by factor kappa: C = kappa * epsilon_0 * A/d. Energy stored: U = (1/2)CV^2 = (1/2)Q^2/C = (1/2)QV. Capacitors in parallel: C_total = C1 + C2 (same voltage, charge adds). Capacitors in series: 1/C_total = 1/C1 + 1/C2 (same charge, voltages add). Students must calculate capacitance, stored energy, and equivalent capacitance for series and parallel combinations. Connect to electric fields, circuits, and RC circuits. AP exam note: capacitor calculations and combinations are tested. Vocabulary: capacitor, capacitance, farad, dielectric, parallel plate, series, parallel, stored energy.

This topic covers conservation of electric energy for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that total energy is conserved for charged particles moving in electric fields: KE_i + U_i = KE_f + U_f (when only electric forces act). A positive charge accelerates from high to low potential (loses PE, gains KE). A negative charge accelerates from low to high potential. The energy gained or lost by a charge moving through a potential difference: delta-KE = q*delta-V. The electron-volt (eV) is a convenient energy unit: 1 eV = 1.6 x 10^-19 J (energy gained by one elementary charge through 1 V). Students must apply energy conservation to determine speeds of charged particles after moving through potential differences. Connect to electric potential, kinetic energy, and particle accelerators. AP exam note: energy conservation calculations with charges in fields are common. Vocabulary: conservation of energy, potential difference, electron-volt, kinetic energy, electric potential energy, accelerating charge.

Electric Circuits.prompt: This topic covers electric current for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that electric current (I) is the rate of charge flow: I = delta-Q/delta-t, measured in amperes (A = C/s). Conventional current direction is the direction positive charges would flow (from high to low potential), opposite to electron flow in metals. Current is the same at all points in a series circuit (charge is conserved, there is no accumulation). In a complete circuit, a battery or power source provides the energy (emf) to drive current through resistive elements. Drift velocity is the average velocity of charge carriers, which is very slow compared to the speed of the electric signal. Students must calculate current and relate it to charge flow. Connect to Ohm's law, circuits, and power. AP exam note: current calculations and understanding of conventional vs. electron flow are foundational. Vocabulary: current, ampere, charge flow, conventional current, electron flow, drift velocity, emf, circuit.

This topic covers simple circuits for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that a simple circuit consists of an energy source (battery with emf), conducting wires, and a load (resistor). The battery maintains a potential difference that drives current through the circuit. In a series circuit, current is the same through all elements and voltages add: V_total = V1 + V2 + .... In a parallel circuit, voltage is the same across all branches and currents add: I_total = I1 + I2 + .... Switches control current flow by opening or closing the circuit. Short circuits occur when current bypasses the intended path through a low-resistance connection. Students must analyze simple series and parallel circuits and calculate voltages and currents. Connect to Ohm's law, Kirchhoff's rules, and power. AP exam note: series and parallel circuit analysis is heavily tested. Vocabulary: series circuit, parallel circuit, emf, battery, resistor, voltage, current, switch, short circuit.

This topic covers resistance, resistivity, and Ohm’s law for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that resistance (R) opposes current flow: R = V/I (Ohm’s law), measured in ohms. Resistance depends on material and geometry: R = rho*L/A, where rho is resistivity, L is length, and A is cross-sectional area. Longer and thinner wires have higher resistance. Resistivity is a material property that generally increases with temperature for metals. Ohmic materials have constant resistance (linear V-I graph); non-ohmic materials (diodes, filament bulbs) have variable resistance. Resistors in series: R_total = R1 + R2. Resistors in parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2. Students must calculate resistance and equivalent resistance, and apply Ohm’s law. AP exam note: Ohm’s law and resistor combination calculations are core skills. Vocabulary: resistance, resistivity, Ohm’s law, ohm, series, parallel, equivalent resistance, conductor, ohmic.

This topic covers electric power for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that electric power is the rate of energy dissipation or delivery: P = IV = I^2*R = V^2/R. Power is measured in watts (W = J/s). In a resistor, electrical energy converts to thermal energy (Joule heating). The total power delivered by a battery is P = emf * I. Energy consumed over time: E = P*t, often measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh) for practical applications. In series circuits, the resistor with highest R dissipates the most power (same current, P = I^2*R). In parallel circuits, the resistor with lowest R dissipates the most power (same voltage, P = V^2/R). Students must calculate power dissipated in individual resistors and total circuits. Connect to Ohm's law, circuit analysis, and energy conservation. AP exam note: power calculations in circuits are commonly tested. Vocabulary: electric power, watt, Joule heating, energy dissipation, kilowatt-hour, power dissipation.

This topic covers compound DC circuits for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand that compound circuits contain both series and parallel combinations of resistors. Analysis strategy: identify series and parallel groups, simplify step-by-step to find equivalent resistance, then work backwards to find individual currents and voltages. The total current from the battery: I_total = emf / R_total. Voltage divides across series elements; current divides across parallel branches. Internal resistance of a battery (r) reduces the terminal voltage: V_terminal = emf - I*r. Students must systematically simplify compound circuits, calculate all currents and voltages, and account for internal resistance. Connect to Kirchhoff's rules, power, and circuit design. AP exam note: compound circuit analysis is a classic free-response problem. Vocabulary: compound circuit, equivalent resistance, terminal voltage, internal resistance, series-parallel combination, simplification.

This topic covers Kirchhoff’s Loop Rule for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand that Kirchhoff’s loop rule (voltage law) states that the sum of all potential differences around any closed loop in a circuit equals zero: sum of delta-V = 0. This is a consequence of conservation of energy. When traversing a loop: voltage increases across a battery (from - to +), decreases across a resistor in the direction of current (V = IR). Multiple loops may be needed for circuits with multiple batteries or complex configurations. Students must choose loop directions, write loop equations with correct signs, and solve simultaneous equations for unknown currents and voltages. Connect to energy conservation, Kirchhoff’s junction rule, and circuit analysis. AP exam note: applying the loop rule to multi-loop circuits is a key analytical skill. Vocabulary: Kirchhoff’s loop rule, voltage law, potential difference, closed loop, conservation of energy, sign convention.

This topic covers Kirchhoff’s Junction Rule for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that Kirchhoff’s junction rule (current law) states that the total current entering a junction equals the total current leaving: sum of I_in = sum of I_out. This is a consequence of conservation of charge (charge does not accumulate at any point). Combined with the loop rule, this provides enough equations to solve any circuit. For a circuit with n unknown currents, you need n independent equations from junctions and loops. Students must identify junctions, write junction equations, and combine them with loop equations to solve complex circuits. Connect to conservation of charge, loop rule, and compound circuits. AP exam note: junction and loop rules together are needed for multi-loop circuit problems. Vocabulary: Kirchhoff’s junction rule, current law, junction, conservation of charge, node, branch current.

This topic covers RC circuits for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand that when a capacitor charges through a resistor, the current starts at maximum (I_0 = emf/R) and decays exponentially: I(t) = I_0 * e^(-t/RC), while voltage across the capacitor grows: V_C(t) = emf*(1 - e^(-t/RC)). The time constant tau = RC determines the charging rate: at t = tau, the capacitor reaches about 63 percent of maximum charge. During discharging, both current and voltage decay exponentially with the same time constant. After 5 time constants, the capacitor is approximately fully charged or discharged. Students must calculate time constants, sketch charging and discharging curves, and determine voltages and currents at specific times. Connect to capacitance, exponential functions, and circuit analysis. AP exam note: RC circuit behavior and time constant calculations are tested. Vocabulary: RC circuit, time constant, exponential decay, charging, discharging, capacitor, resistor, tau.

Magnetism and Electromagnetism.prompt: This topic covers magnetic fields for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that magnetic fields (B) are produced by moving charges, including current-carrying wires and permanent magnets. Magnetic field lines form closed loops: they exit the north pole and enter the south pole of a magnet. Unlike electric monopoles, magnetic monopoles have never been observed; magnetic poles always come in pairs. A long straight wire carrying current I produces a magnetic field: B = mu_0*I/(2*pi*r), with direction given by the right-hand rule (thumb along current, fingers curl in field direction). Inside a solenoid: B = mu_0*nI, where n is turns per unit length. Students must calculate magnetic fields and determine directions using the right-hand rule. Connect to forces on charges, electromagnetic induction, and Ampere's law concepts. AP exam note: right-hand rule applications are essential. Vocabulary: magnetic field, field lines, north pole, south pole, right-hand rule, tesla, mu_0, solenoid.

This topic covers the magnetic force on moving charges for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that a magnetic field exerts a force on a moving charge: F = qvB*sin(theta), where theta is the angle between v and B. The direction is given by the right-hand rule (for positive charges): point fingers in v direction, curl toward B, thumb points in F direction (reverse for negative charges). The magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity, so it does no work and cannot change speed, only direction. A charge moving perpendicular to a uniform B field travels in a circle with radius r = mv/(qB). This principle underlies mass spectrometers, cyclotrons, and the aurora. Students must calculate forces, determine directions, and analyze circular motion of charges in fields. AP exam note: right-hand rule and circular motion in B fields are heavily tested. Vocabulary: magnetic force, Lorentz force, right-hand rule, circular motion, perpendicular, centripetal, mass spectrometer.

This topic covers magnetism and current-carrying wires for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field experiences a force: F = BIL*sin(theta), where I is current, L is wire length in the field, and theta is the angle between the wire and B. The direction follows the right-hand rule (point fingers along current, curl toward B, thumb gives force direction). Two parallel wires carrying current exert forces on each other: same-direction currents attract, opposite-direction currents repel (each wire's field exerts a force on the other wire's current). The force per unit length between parallel wires: F/L = mu_0*I1*I2/(2*pi*d). This principle defines the ampere. Students must calculate forces on wires and determine directions. Connect to motors, torque on current loops, and electromagnetic induction. AP exam note: force on wires in fields is a core skill. Vocabulary: force on wire, current-carrying wire, right-hand rule, parallel wires, motor, torque, magnetic field.

This topic covers electromagnetic induction and Faraday’s law for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that a changing magnetic flux through a conducting loop induces an emf (voltage): Faraday’s law states emf = -d(phi_B)/dt, where magnetic flux phi_B = BA*cos(theta). The negative sign reflects Lenz’s law: the induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the change in flux that caused it (conservation of energy). Flux changes occur when B, A, or the angle theta changes. Applications include generators (rotating coils in magnetic fields), transformers, and eddy currents. For a coil with N turns: emf = -N*d(phi_B)/dt. Students must calculate induced emf, determine the direction of induced current using Lenz’s law, and analyze scenarios causing flux changes. Connect to magnetic fields, circuits, and energy conservation. AP exam note: Faraday’s law and Lenz’s law are heavily tested. Vocabulary: electromagnetic induction, Faraday’s law, magnetic flux, Lenz’s law, induced emf, induced current, generator, transformer.

Geometric Optics.prompt: This topic covers reflection for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand the law of reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection (theta_i = theta_r), both measured from the normal to the surface. Specular reflection occurs on smooth surfaces and produces clear images; diffuse reflection occurs on rough surfaces and scatters light in many directions. Light rays are reversible: a ray traveling from A to B follows the same path from B to A. Reflection preserves the angle and wavelength of light. Plane mirrors produce virtual images that are the same size as the object, upright, and located the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front. Students must apply the law of reflection and locate images formed by plane mirrors. Connect to mirrors, image formation, and wave behavior. AP exam note: ray diagram construction for plane mirrors is foundational. Vocabulary: reflection, angle of incidence, angle of reflection, normal, specular, diffuse, virtual image, plane mirror.

This topic covers images formed by curved mirrors for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand that concave mirrors converge parallel rays to a focal point (f = R/2, where R is the radius of curvature) and convex mirrors diverge them (virtual focal point behind the mirror). The mirror equation: 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i, where d_o is object distance and d_i is image distance. Magnification: M = -d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o. Sign conventions: positive d_i for real images (in front), negative for virtual (behind); positive M for upright, negative for inverted. Students must use ray diagrams (parallel, focal, and center rays) and the mirror equation to locate and characterize images (real/virtual, upright/inverted, magnified/reduced). Connect to reflection, lenses, and optical instruments. AP exam note: ray diagrams and mirror equation calculations are heavily tested. Vocabulary: concave mirror, convex mirror, focal point, focal length, real image, virtual image, magnification, mirror equation.

This topic covers refraction for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand that refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another due to a change in speed. Snell's law: n1*sin(theta_1) = n2*sin(theta_2), where n is the index of refraction (n = c/v). Light slows down and bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (higher n) and speeds up and bends away from the normal when entering a less dense medium. Total internal reflection occurs when light travels from a denser to a less dense medium at an angle greater than the critical angle: theta_c = arcsin(n2/n1). Applications include fiber optics, prisms, and diamonds. Students must apply Snell's law and calculate critical angles. Connect to lenses, dispersion, and wave behavior. AP exam note: Snell's law calculations and total internal reflection are commonly tested. Vocabulary: refraction, Snell's law, index of refraction, normal, total internal reflection, critical angle, denser medium.

This topic covers images formed by lenses for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that converging (convex) lenses focus parallel light to a real focal point, while diverging (concave) lenses spread light as if from a virtual focal point. The thin lens equation: 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i, with the same form as the mirror equation. Sign conventions: positive f for converging, negative for diverging; positive d_i for real images (opposite side from object), negative for virtual (same side as object). Magnification: M = -d_i/d_o. Ray diagrams use three principal rays: parallel (refracts through focal point), through center (undeviated), and through focal point (refracts parallel). Students must draw ray diagrams and solve lens equation problems. Connect to refraction, mirrors, and optical instruments (microscopes, telescopes). AP exam note: lens problems and ray diagrams are heavily tested. Vocabulary: converging lens, diverging lens, focal length, real image, virtual image, thin lens equation, magnification, ray diagram.

Waves, Sound, and Physical Optics.skill: Properties of Wave Pulses and Waves
Waves, Sound, and Physical Optics.prompt: This topic covers properties of wave pulses and waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that waves transfer energy without transferring matter. A wave pulse is a single disturbance; a continuous wave is a repeated periodic disturbance. Transverse waves have particle displacement perpendicular to wave propagation (electromagnetic waves, waves on strings). Longitudinal waves have particle displacement parallel to propagation (sound waves). Key properties include amplitude (maximum displacement), wavelength (distance between consecutive identical points), frequency (oscillations per second), and wave speed (v = f*lambda). Wave speed depends on the medium, not on frequency or amplitude. Students must classify wave types and calculate wave properties. Connect to superposition, standing waves, and electromagnetic waves. AP exam note: wave property relationships are foundational. Vocabulary: transverse wave, longitudinal wave, amplitude, wavelength, frequency, wave speed, pulse, medium.

This topic covers periodic waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that periodic waves are generated by periodic sources and have constant frequency, wavelength, and amplitude in a uniform medium. The wave equation v = f*lambda relates speed, frequency, and wavelength. Period T = 1/f is the time for one complete cycle. For waves on a string: v = sqrt(F_tension/mu), where mu is linear mass density. For sound in air: v depends on temperature (approximately 343 m/s at 20 degrees C). Students must interpret wave diagrams showing displacement vs. position (wavelength visible) and displacement vs. time (period visible), and distinguish between the two graph types. Connect to wave speed, standing waves, and musical instruments. AP exam note: interpreting wave graphs and applying v = f*lambda are core skills. Vocabulary: periodic wave, frequency, wavelength, period, wave speed, wave equation, displacement graph, continuous wave.

Waves, Sound, and Physical Optics.skill: Boundary Behavior of Waves and Polarization
Waves, Sound, and Physical Optics.prompt: This topic covers boundary behavior of waves and polarization for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that when a wave reaches a boundary between media, part is reflected and part is transmitted. A wave reflecting from a fixed end (denser medium) inverts; from a free end (less dense medium) it reflects upright. When entering a denser medium, wavelength decreases (frequency stays constant, speed decreases). Polarization applies only to transverse waves: a polarizer transmits only the component of oscillation along its transmission axis. Malus's law: I = I_0*cos^2(theta), where theta is the angle between the polarization direction and the transmission axis. Unpolarized light passing through a polarizer loses half its intensity. Students must analyze boundary reflections and calculate transmitted intensity through polarizers. AP exam note: polarization and boundary behavior are tested. Vocabulary: reflection, transmission, fixed end, free end, inversion, polarization, polarizer, Malus's law, transverse wave.

This topic covers electromagnetic waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that electromagnetic (EM) waves are transverse waves consisting of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation. All EM waves travel at the speed of light in vacuum: c = 3.0 x 10^8 m/s. The EM spectrum ranges from radio waves (lowest frequency, longest wavelength) through microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, to gamma rays (highest frequency, shortest wavelength). EM waves require no medium and carry energy proportional to frequency: E = hf. Students must relate frequency, wavelength, and speed for EM waves and order them on the spectrum. Connect to photon energy, spectroscopy, and wave-particle duality. AP exam note: EM spectrum ordering and c = f*lambda calculations are tested. Vocabulary: electromagnetic wave, speed of light, electric field, magnetic field, EM spectrum, transverse, photon, frequency.

This topic covers the Doppler effect for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that the Doppler effect is the change in observed frequency (and wavelength) of a wave due to relative motion between the source and observer. When the source and observer approach each other, the observed frequency increases (higher pitch for sound, blue shift for light). When they move apart, the observed frequency decreases (lower pitch, red shift). For sound: f_observed = f_source * (v +/- v_observer) / (v -/+ v_source), where v is the wave speed in the medium. The effect depends on the velocity relative to the medium. For light, the relativistic Doppler formula applies but is not required for AP Physics 2. Students must predict frequency shifts and solve Doppler problems qualitatively and quantitatively. Connect to wave properties, sound, and astronomy. AP exam note: qualitative Doppler effect reasoning is commonly tested. Vocabulary: Doppler effect, frequency shift, blue shift, red shift, approaching, receding, observer, source.

This topic covers wave interference and standing waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that when two waves overlap, they superpose: constructive interference occurs when waves are in phase (crests align, amplitudes add) and destructive interference occurs when waves are out of phase (crest meets trough, amplitudes cancel). Standing waves form when two waves of equal frequency and amplitude travel in opposite directions. Standing waves have nodes (zero displacement, destructive interference) and antinodes (maximum displacement, constructive interference). For a string fixed at both ends: harmonics occur at f_n = n*v/(2L). For an open pipe: same formula. For a closed pipe (one end closed): f_n = n*v/(4L), n = odd integers only. Students must identify harmonics and calculate resonant frequencies. AP exam note: standing wave patterns and harmonic calculations are heavily tested. Vocabulary: interference, constructive, destructive, standing wave, node, antinode, harmonic, resonance, fundamental frequency.

This topic covers diffraction for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Students should understand that diffraction is the bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through openings. Significant diffraction occurs when the opening size is comparable to the wavelength. Single-slit diffraction produces a central maximum wider than the slit with dimmer secondary maxima on either side. The condition for minima in single-slit diffraction: a*sin(theta) = m*lambda, where a is the slit width and m = 1, 2, 3, .... Narrower slits produce wider diffraction patterns. The central maximum contains most of the energy and is twice as wide as secondary maxima. Students must predict diffraction patterns and calculate positions of minima. Connect to interference, wave properties, and the wave nature of light. AP exam note: single-slit diffraction patterns and calculations are tested. Vocabulary: diffraction, single slit, central maximum, secondary maximum, minimum, slit width, wavelength, spreading.

This topic covers double-slit interference and diffraction gratings for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.8). Students should understand that in Young's double-slit experiment, coherent light passing through two slits produces an interference pattern of bright and dark fringes. Constructive interference (bright fringes): d*sin(theta) = m*lambda (m = 0, 1, 2, ...). Destructive interference (dark fringes): d*sin(theta) = (m + 1/2)*lambda. The fringe spacing on a screen: delta-y = lambda*L/d. A diffraction grating has many slits and produces sharper, more defined maxima at the same angular positions. Greater slit number produces sharper peaks. Students must calculate fringe positions, angular separations, and wavelengths from interference data. Connect to wave nature of light, single-slit diffraction, and spectroscopy. AP exam note: double-slit calculations and diffraction grating analysis are heavily tested. Vocabulary: double slit, interference pattern, bright fringe, dark fringe, path difference, diffraction grating, order, coherent light.

This topic covers thin-film interference for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.9). Students should understand that when light reflects from the top and bottom surfaces of a thin transparent film, the two reflected beams can interfere constructively or destructively depending on the film thickness, wavelength, and refractive indices. A phase shift of 180 degrees (half wavelength) occurs upon reflection from a denser medium (higher n). The effective wavelength in the film is lambda_film = lambda_vacuum/n_film. For constructive interference (accounting for phase shifts): 2*n*t = (m + 1/2)*lambda when one phase shift occurs, or 2*n*t = m*lambda when zero or two phase shifts occur. Applications include antireflection coatings, soap bubbles, and oil films on water. Students must determine conditions for constructive and destructive interference in thin films. AP exam note: thin-film problems require careful tracking of phase shifts. Vocabulary: thin-film interference, phase shift, optical path length, constructive, destructive, antireflection coating, refractive index.

Modern Physics.prompt: This topic covers quantum theory and wave-particle duality for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand that light exhibits both wave properties (diffraction, interference) and particle properties (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering). Matter also shows wave-particle duality: the de Broglie wavelength of a particle is lambda = h/p = h/(mv), where h is Planck's constant. This wave nature is significant only for very small particles (electrons, not macroscopic objects). Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that position and momentum cannot both be known precisely simultaneously: delta-x * delta-p >= h/(4*pi). Students must calculate de Broglie wavelengths and understand when wave behavior of matter is observable. Connect to photoelectric effect, atomic structure, and diffraction. AP exam note: de Broglie wavelength calculations and understanding duality are tested. Vocabulary: wave-particle duality, de Broglie wavelength, Planck's constant, photon, matter wave, uncertainty principle, quantum.

Modern Physics.skill: The Bohr Model of Atomic Structure
Modern Physics.prompt: This topic covers the Bohr model of atomic structure for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand that Bohr's model of the hydrogen atom postulates that electrons orbit the nucleus in quantized energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3, ...) with energies E_n = -13.6/n^2 eV. Electrons can only transition between levels by absorbing or emitting photons with energy exactly equal to the energy difference: E_photon = E_higher - E_lower = hf. The ground state (n = 1) is the lowest energy level; excited states have higher n values. Ionization occurs when an electron absorbs enough energy to reach E = 0. While the Bohr model is limited to hydrogen-like atoms and has been superseded by quantum mechanics, its energy level concept remains fundamental. Students must calculate energy transitions and corresponding photon wavelengths. AP exam note: energy level calculations are commonly tested. Vocabulary: Bohr model, energy level, quantum number, ground state, excited state, ionization, transition, quantized.

This topic covers emission and absorption spectra for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand that when atoms transition from higher to lower energy levels, they emit photons at specific frequencies, producing an emission spectrum of bright lines. When white light passes through a gas, atoms absorb photons at the same specific frequencies, producing an absorption spectrum of dark lines at the same positions. Each element has a unique spectral fingerprint determined by its energy level structure. The photon energy equals the energy level difference: E = hf = hc/lambda. Students must interpret emission and absorption spectra, calculate transition energies and wavelengths, and use spectra to identify elements. Connect to the Bohr model, quantum theory, and stellar composition analysis. AP exam note: interpreting spectra and calculating photon energies from energy level diagrams are tested. Vocabulary: emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, spectral lines, energy level transition, photon energy, element identification.

This topic covers blackbody radiation for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand that a blackbody is an idealized object that absorbs and emits all frequencies of electromagnetic radiation. The spectrum of blackbody radiation depends only on temperature: hotter objects emit more total radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann law: P = sigma*A*T^4) and their peak wavelength shifts to shorter wavelengths (Wien's displacement law: lambda_max*T = 2.898 x 10^-3 m*K). Classical physics failed to explain the blackbody spectrum (ultraviolet catastrophe). Planck resolved this by proposing that energy is quantized: E = nhf, launching quantum physics. Students must use Wien's law to relate temperature to peak wavelength and understand Planck's quantum hypothesis. Connect to quantum theory, photons, and stellar classification. AP exam note: Wien's law calculations and understanding the historical importance of blackbody radiation are tested. Vocabulary: blackbody radiation, Wien's displacement law, Stefan-Boltzmann law, ultraviolet catastrophe, Planck, quantized energy, peak wavelength.

This topic covers the photoelectric effect for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.5). Students should understand that when light of sufficient frequency shines on a metal surface, electrons are ejected. Key observations that support the photon model: (1) there is a minimum threshold frequency below which no electrons are ejected regardless of intensity, (2) increasing intensity increases the number of ejected electrons but not their maximum kinetic energy, (3) increasing frequency above threshold increases maximum KE. Einstein's equation: KE_max = hf - phi, where phi is the work function (minimum energy to eject an electron). The stopping potential V_s satisfies eV_s = KE_max. Students must calculate threshold frequency, KE_max, and stopping potential, and explain why the wave model fails. AP exam note: photoelectric effect analysis is heavily tested. Vocabulary: photoelectric effect, work function, threshold frequency, photon, stopping potential, intensity, kinetic energy, Einstein.

This topic covers Compton scattering for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.6). Students should understand that Compton scattering occurs when a photon collides with an electron, transferring some of its energy and momentum. The scattered photon has a longer wavelength (lower energy) than the incident photon, and the electron recoils with the transferred kinetic energy. The wavelength shift depends on the scattering angle: delta-lambda = (h/(m_e*c))*(1 - cos(theta)), where h/(m_e*c) = 2.43 x 10^-12 m is the Compton wavelength of the electron. This effect demonstrates that photons carry momentum: p = h/lambda = E/c. Maximum energy transfer occurs in head-on collisions (180-degree scattering). Students must calculate wavelength shifts and understand Compton scattering as evidence for photon momentum. Connect to wave-particle duality, conservation of energy and momentum, and photoelectric effect. AP exam note: Compton scattering reinforces the particle nature of light. Vocabulary: Compton scattering, wavelength shift, photon momentum, recoil electron, Compton wavelength, scattering angle.

This topic covers fission, fusion, and nuclear decay for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.7). Students should understand that nuclear reactions involve changes in the nucleus and release or absorb enormous energies described by E = mc^2 (mass-energy equivalence). Nuclear fission splits heavy nuclei (e.g., U-235) into lighter fragments, releasing energy and neutrons (chain reactions in reactors and weapons). Nuclear fusion combines light nuclei (e.g., hydrogen to helium), releasing even more energy per nucleon (powers stars). Both processes move nuclei toward the region of maximum binding energy per nucleon (iron-56). The mass defect (difference between constituent nucleon masses and actual nuclear mass) corresponds to binding energy. Students must compare fission and fusion, calculate energy released using mass defect, and interpret binding energy curves. AP exam note: binding energy per nucleon curves and energy calculations are tested. Vocabulary: fission, fusion, mass defect, binding energy, chain reaction, mass-energy equivalence, nucleon, E = mc^2.

This topic covers types of radioactive decay for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Students should understand the three main types of radioactive decay. Alpha decay: nucleus emits an alpha particle (He-4 nucleus, 2 protons + 2 neutrons), reducing atomic number by 2 and mass number by 4. Beta-minus decay: a neutron converts to a proton, emitting an electron and antineutrino, increasing atomic number by 1. Beta-plus decay (positron emission): a proton converts to a neutron, emitting a positron and neutrino, decreasing atomic number by 1. Gamma decay: nucleus releases energy as a gamma photon with no change in atomic or mass number. All decays conserve charge, mass number, and energy. Radioactive decay follows first-order kinetics: N(t) = N_0 * (1/2)^(t/t_half). Students must write balanced nuclear equations and calculate remaining nuclei after given time periods. AP exam note: writing decay equations and half-life calculations are tested. Vocabulary: alpha decay, beta decay, gamma decay, half-life, radioactive decay, nuclear equation, conservation laws, positron.
